chapters.title: Elizabeth Holmes was a competitive child
chapters.text:
Elizabeth Anne Holmes had big ambitions from a young age. By seven, she was sketching a time machine and dreaming of being a businesswoman. In 2002, she entered Stanford as a President Scholar at eighteen.

Holmes lobbied her way into a research lab run by Channing Robertson, the most prominent figure in Stanford chemical engineering department. She dropped out of Stanford after an internship at the Genome Institute of Singapore, where she had tested patients using syringes and swabs.

She patented an arm patch that could both diagnose and treat. She was determined to draw on her classes with Robertson and her internship experience. She recruited Shaunak Roy, Robertson Ph.D. student, as her first employee when the company launched.

Ambition is fine, as long as it does not put other people lives at risk.

Holmes set up operations in Burlingame, California, and later moved to an industrial zone in Menlo Park. She leaned on family connections and persuaded Tim Draper, a former neighbor, to invest a million dollars in her firm.

As John Carreyrou has written, like her idol Steve Jobs, she projected a reality distortion field that made people momentarily suspend disbelief.

By the end of 2004, Holmes had raised six million dollars from investors. Shaunak felt they were hitting their first milestones after eighteen months of work. The company launched the Theranos 1.0 prototype, hired twenty three more staff, and was named the hottest startup in the valley by Red Herring magazine.

This summary walks through how a rising company sank to the bottom, the dangers of leadership without accountability, and how truth telling can save lives.

chapters.title: The first successful Theranos prototype
chapters.text:
Holmes brought in Edmond Ku to run engineering at Theranos. Ku had earned a reputation as a Mr. Fixit in the valley.

Holmes pushed Ku to run his department around the clock, but he pushed back, saying engineers needed rest to perform well. Holmes eventually set up a rival engineering group under Tony Nugent, who redesigned the Theranos by stripping out Ku microfluidics process. The new design, called the Edison, was a success.

Pay attention to both ends and means if you want success to last.

Her admiration for Apple led Holmes to meet Ana Arriola, who agreed to join as the chief designer. Arriola soon realized that the blood testing device was far from complete.

Arriola reached out to Avie Tevanian, a shareholder at Theranos. Tevanian saw that the revenue projections were unrealistic. Research confirmed his suspicions, and he left the company.

The move to Palo Alto in 2008 marked a new chapter at Theranos. The trials being run were in desperate need of improvement. Holmes saw the Tennessee trial was heading for failure and turned instead to the Swiss giant Novartis. The devices performed terribly in Switzerland, and Holmes wrote it off as a technical glitch.

Todd Surdey, the sales executive, brought in Michael Esquivel, Theranos general counsel, and worked with a board member, Tom Brodeen, to destabilize the board. The group agreed to replace the CEO with Brodeen. Holmes asked for a second chance to lead the company, promising accountability and transparency, then fired Surdey and Esquivel weeks after regaining power.

Holding a grudge does more harm than good in business and in life.

Holmes parents were neighbors with the entrepreneur and medical inventor Richard Fuisz, who had made a fortune selling medical patents.

On a trip, Holmes mother told Fuisz wife about her daughter progress at Theranos. Richard was annoyed that Holmes felt his expertise in medical patents was unnecessary, and he worked with his lawyer Alan Schiavelli to file a patent that exploited weaknesses he saw in Theranos. Gary Frenzel, head of the chemistry team, did not find out about this patent until eighteen months after it was filed, in January 2008.

Holmes decided to sue Richard Fuisz. She approached Ken Page of McDermott, the firm where she had originally registered her patent. However, Fuisz son John was a partner at the firm. McDermott dropped the suit, reasoning that suing a partner parent was unwise.

Before Elizabeth Holmes settled on the name the Edison, Theranos employees used to call the prototype gluebot, because it looked like a glue dispensing robot.

chapters.title: Ramesh Balwani: the Indian hire who turned up the heat
chapters.text:
Chelsea Burkett had been close friends with Holmes since they met at Stanford. They went to parties together and kept in touch after Holmes dropped out. Unhappy with her job, she reached out to Holmes, who pitched her on Theranos. Burkett agreed to join.

Theranos hired Burkett to work with the validation studies group, but the arrival of Ramesh Sunny Balwani made her role complicated. Balwani had made about forty one million dollars from a commercial deal in the late nineties. In the unexplained role of vice executive chairman, he kept the staff at Theranos on edge.

Seeing an important Pfizer deal about to slip away, Holmes sent Burkett and Daniel Young, a Theranos executive she had hired to add a new dimension to the blood testing system, to Belgium to pitch the Edison. The device kept throwing error messages, and the trip was a flop. Holmes saw similar results when trying to assess the spread of swine flu in Mexico.

As John Carreyrou has written, Elizabeth was so persuasive. She had this intense way of looking at you while she spoke that made you believe in her and want to follow her.

Burkett grew frustrated with these problems, and Balwani kept terrorizing the staff. He fired Seth Michelson, an engineer from NASA, and threatened to have Tony Nugent removed. Burkett quit when Holmes recommended the Edison, which was still far from ready, for a cancer patient diagnosis.

There should be a clear line between profit and the safety of human lives, and entrepreneurs should not cross it.

Holmes approached Jay Rosan, known as Dr. J, of Walgreens. Excited by the Edison, other Walgreens executives, including Kevin Hunter, Jim Sundberg, and Renaat Van Den Hooff, met with Theranos and pledged to invest. Both sides also agreed to use the Edison for their tests.

Suspicious of the unwillingness to release blood test results, Hunter asked Dr. J and discovered that Johns Hopkins had not certified the Edison the way Theranos claimed. Theranos had falsified the number of blood samples their cartridges could handle.

chapters.title: Holmes legal fights with the Fuisz family
chapters.text:
In October 2011, Holmes decided to take the fight to Fuisz. She sued Richard Fuisz for conspiring with his children, Joe and John, to steal confidential patent information and file a competing patent. Holmes hired David Boies to handle the case. Boies had made a name for himself in the nineties when the Department of Justice brought him in on a case against Microsoft. He reportedly deposed Bill Gates for more than twenty hours.

Citing a case from the nineties, Boies argued that it was a pattern for John Fuisz to funnel stolen information to his father. Under deposition, John performed poorly. Boies ended John career with legal maneuvers. Searching for evidence to strengthen his case, Richard Fuisz came across a scientist named Ian Gibbons, but Boies blocked Fuisz from reaching out to him.

Watch out for a person character. They can make or break your career and your life.

Theranos claimed they could run more than eight hundred tests on a single blood sample and that their methods were more accurate than traditional approaches. TBWA, the advertising agency, requested a nondisclosure on the website to avoid liability for misleading ads. Knowing she could not get away with exaggerated claims, Holmes agreed to modify the website content two days before its launch in April 2013.

David Boies wore down Richard Fuisz and his sons. After spending more than two million dollars, the Fuiszes decided to go pro se by letting Joe Fuisz handle the case. Boies took him apart. The Fuiszes further damaged their case with conflicting statements during depositions.

In March 2014, Richard and Boies met to settle. Fuisz agreed to withdraw his patent, and Theranos agreed to drop its suit. Frustrated, John Fuisz hired Julia Love of the American Lawyer Media, ALM, to publish an article. He claimed Boies had bribed the other Fuiszes and threatened to sue them.

Roger Parloff of Fortune magazine came across the ALM article and decided to meet Holmes. After a seven hour interview, Parloff was swept up in the vision of Theranos. In his next piece, titled The CEO Out for Blood, he described Holmes and her dream of saving the world with a single drop of blood.

chapters.title: Richard Fuisz accidentally made Holmes richer
chapters.text:
Media exposure was Holmes grand entrance into stardom. Major outlets including Forbes came calling. Everyone wanted a closer look at the company valued at nine billion dollars. Barack Obama made Holmes an ambassador, and Theranos had to upgrade her security detail.

When you build your life on a lie, you will come tumbling down no matter how high you rise.

Alan Beam grew tired of the schemes at Theranos. He could not access the Control Test data and voiced his concerns in a meeting with the scientist Curtis Schneider.

Beam began moving emails he had shared with Balwani and Young to his personal Gmail account. He resigned in December 2014.

Balwani found the emails and forced him to delete them after Beam signed an affidavit. Beam refused, and Theranos threatened to sue him. On his lawyer advice, Beam eventually deleted the one hundred seventy five emails.

Adam Clapper, a pathologist at Columbia, was convinced that tiny drops of blood would not work the way Theranos claimed. Research also showed that no study had been published to support the company claims. When Beam met with Clapper, Richard Fuisz, Joe Fuisz, and others, he described practices at Theranos that betrayed the Hippocratic Oath.

Beam forwarded his findings to John Carreyrou of the Wall Street Journal in February 2015. Carreyrou spoke with Beam on February 26, 2015, and learned that employees had been leaving the firm for similar reasons. Many were not willing to break confidentiality, but Tyler Schultz, Erika Cheung, and others eventually came forward.

Beam said that in some clinics, Theranos was not even using its own technology. Instead, it was using Siemens machines.

Several doctors who had used the Edison reported that it constantly failed. Aware of the scale of the opponent, Carreyrou pulled his findings together and prepared to publish.

chapters.title: Theranos sent threatening emails trying to silence people
chapters.text:
Tyler Schultz soon drew heavy fire from Theranos. The company sued Schultz for sharing confidential information with Carreyrou. Carreyrou eventually set up a meeting with David Boies after several failed attempts to reach both Schultz and Holmes. They scheduled it for June 11, 2015.

Boies came to the meeting with Mike Brille, Meredith Dearborn, Daniel Young, and King Heather. Expecting a crossfire, Carreyrou brought Jay Conti and Mike Siconolfi, who headed the investigation team.

The meeting started with Heather remarks to rebut the false premises. Boies kept his cool at first while King and Brille did most of the talking. Daniel Young acknowledged that Theranos owned and used other devices, but said it was for comparison rather than operations. When asked if Siemens was one of them, he called it a trade secret.

On the Edison, Young admitted it had flaws but said the company had moved past them. He said the number of tests it could run was a trade secret. Theranos refused to share details of their patents since Carreyrou would not sign a nondisclosure agreement. After the meeting, Theranos wrote to Carreyrou. They threatened to sue him if he published anything they considered a trade secret or defamatory.

In July 2015, the FDA approved Theranos testing of the HSV 1 project, allowing a real look at whether the technology was effective.

In early July 2015, the State of Arizona passed a bill that allowed people to test their blood without a doctor order.

Theranos installed its devices in Walgreens stores but did not commercialize them. The plan was to stay in the public eye without drawing heat from the FDA. The clinics fitted with the miniLabs did collect blood samples, but they processed them with other blood analyzers.

The level of deception kept rising. Theranos hired Michael Craig to mask code failure in the miniLab. Holmes and Balwani designed a fake lab space for a visit by Joe Biden.

Carreyrou worked the case with Wall Street Journal senior editor Mike Siconolfi. Holmes appeared at the annual Wall Street Journal Technology Conference in November as the guest speaker. Siconolfi suggested they wait and catch the prey off guard. None of the key witnesses were willing to come forward before then.

chapters.title: Thorough research eventually revealed the truth
chapters.text:
Holmes leaned on Rupert Murdoch, who had already invested one hundred fifty million dollars in Theranos, to kill the rumors. Murdoch was a shareholder at the Fox Broadcasting Network.

Theranos arranged a meeting with the Editor in Chief, Gerry Baker, who insisted that Mike Siconolfi, John Carreyrou, and Neal Lipschutz be present. During the meeting, Boies admitted that some tests had not been run on the company proprietary device, contradicting what Young had said, and organized an internal demonstration for Carreyrou. Expecting the demonstration to be delayed for weeks, Carreyrou moved ahead with publication.

On October 15, 2015, the Wall Street Journal published an article titled A Prized Startup Struggle, aimed directly at Theranos. The news shook everything, pulling people onto different sides of the case. Boies published a response claiming Carreyrou had falsified the allegations and threatened a suit.

To deliver the final blow, Carreyrou needed to find out what the Center for Medicaid Services, CMS, had observed during its inspection and regulation test at Theranos.

Cheung tip to Gary Yamamoto, a veteran CMS field inspector, set the agency on a path to inspect the Theranos labs. In her letter to Yamamoto, Cheung listed that the company was guilty of scientific misconduct and sloppy practices and had deceived the initial inspector who came in the fall of 2013. Acting on this information, another CMS field inspector, Sarah Bernett, and Yamamoto set out to visit the Theranos labs. Litigation and bureaucracy slowed them down. After the inspection, CMS gave Theranos time to fix up the labs. Incriminating publications led CMS to inspect again. This time, the secrets of Theranos unraveled. King Heather threatened to sue CMS if the inspection reports were released.

When NBC Today interviewed Holmes, she said she was sorry and unhappy with the state of things.

It may take a while, but the truth always catches up and exposes a lie.

As investors pulled out, Boies also abandoned Theranos. Lawsuits followed, forcing Holmes to shut down the labs. Theranos had to pay four point six five million dollars in refunds to seventy six thousand two hundred seventeen customers.

Had Theranos expanded to all eight thousand one hundred thirty four Walgreens stores in the United States, many more people would have faced grave danger from false diagnoses. Wrong medical treatment would have multiplied.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Elizabeth Holmes set out to revolutionize the medical industry. Her invention was supposed to transform diagnostics. Instead, she became infamous for running a large scale scam. She built her empire on lies. Her promises drew in investors, and the customers who trusted her paid the price in their health, because the promises were never kept.

Aspirations are not wrong, but we cannot put other people lives at risk in pursuit of our dreams. We have to stay human and treat people as equals. Elizabeth Holmes let the power go to her head. She hired a team of yes men and fired anyone who tried to show her the reality she was in. Aspiring leaders should learn from this story. Having people around you who can tell you the truth and question your beliefs is essential. They keep you grounded.

Most importantly, you have to be kind. Walking over people might get you somewhere, but you will not stay there for long. Your methods matter as much as your goals. And once the truth comes to light, the fall comes soon. Dishonest athletes lose their medals, and lying CEOs go to jail.

It takes courage to expose fraud, especially when billions of dollars are at stake. A person with a strong value system, though, will have little trouble choosing to pursue the truth. What are your values? Take some time to write down the character traits that matter most to you, no matter what you do or where you are.

Keep reading to the end.
